charging_networks.description: High-power DC fast-charging network across European motorways, frequently used on this blog's long-distance trips.
Yellow-canopy DC fast-charging network focused on high-speed chargers along major routes in Europe.
German-based fast-charging network with a mix of urban and motorway locations.
Selected Supercharger locations that support non-Tesla vehicles, occasionally used when compatible.

faq_articles.title: Maximum AC Charging Power for Our Test Vehicle
faq_articles.content:
Our primary test car on this blog is the "Test EV 77 kWh". When charging on AC (Type 2) stations, the car supports a maximum AC charging power of 11 kW. This assumes a three-phase 16 A connection.

In practical terms, you can expect the following:
- From 10% to 80% on a 11 kW AC charger: roughly 5.5–6 hours
- From 0% to 100% on a 11 kW AC charger: roughly 7.5–8 hours

The in-car and app settings allow you to cap AC charging power below 11 kW, but they cannot increase it beyond 11 kW. When configuring your profile on this site, select the vehicle model "Test EV 77 kWh" and set the preferred AC charging limit to 11 kW if you want our planner to match the real-world maximum.

faq_articles.title: AC vs DC Charging: What Matters on a Road Trip
faq_articles.content:
On this blog you will see both AC and DC charging sessions in the logs. AC charging is typically slower (up to the vehicle's onboard charger limit, such as 11 kW for the Test EV 77 kWh), but can be ideal for overnight stops or long meal breaks. DC charging bypasses the onboard AC charger and can reach 100–250+ kW depending on the car and charger.

For planning purposes, I rely primarily on DC chargers for trips where I drive more than 300 km per day, and use AC as a backup at hotels, apartments, or destination chargers.

faq_articles.title: How I Compare Ionity and Fastned Pricing on My Trips
faq_articles.content:
Ionity and Fastned are the two most common high-power networks in my logs. To compare pricing, I focus on average price per kWh per log, not per session. The Daily Logs section shows this value under "Avg price per kWh".

Ionity pricing varies a lot depending on country and membership. Fastned is often simpler, with clear per-kWh pricing plus optional subscriptions. When I say one network was "cheaper" in a given comparison, I always refer to the average price per kWh for that specific day, not a global statement.

faq_articles.title: How to Set Your Preferred Charging Network in Your Profile
faq_articles.content:
You can set your preferred charging network so the site highlights matching logs and chargers.

1. Open the user menu in the header and click "Profile & Vehicle".
2. Scroll to the "Charging Preferences" section.
3. Use the "Preferred Charging Network" dropdown to choose between Ionity, Fastned, or "No preference".
4. Click "Save Preferences".

This preference is used to:
- Highlight matching daily logs
- Prioritize chargers from your preferred network in trip suggestions
- Pre-fill filters on the Daily Logs page.

faq_articles.title: Setting Up Your Vehicle Profile
faq_articles.content:
Your vehicle profile determines range estimates, recommended charging power, and the filters used across the site.

Steps:
1. Go to "Profile & Vehicle" in the footer or header.
2. Select your vehicle model (for example, "Test EV 77 kWh" if you want to follow the blog's reference car).
3. Adjust your usable battery capacity if you prefer a conservative buffer.
4. Configure AC and DC charging limits.
5. Save your changes.

You can update this at any time if you switch cars or experiment with different assumptions.

faq_articles.title: What "Winter Efficiency" Means on This Blog
faq_articles.content:
The "Winter Efficiency" tag is applied to trips where the focus is on driving efficiently in cold conditions (typically below 0°C, often below -5°C). These posts highlight:
- Energy consumption in kWh/100 km
- Impact of snow, slush, and heater use
- Preconditioning strategies
- Charger reliability in sub-zero temperatures.

On the Winter Efficiency tag page you can filter by month, temperature, and maximum consumption to find the most relevant logs for your needs.

faq_articles.title: Understanding the Daily Logs Section
faq_articles.content:
The Daily Logs section contains one entry per driving day, including:
- Distance driven
- Number of charging stops and total cost
- Average consumption and speed
- Dominant charging network for that day.

You can filter by country, cost, network, and more. This is the best place to analyze real-world costs or replicate specific days as test routes.

faq_articles.title: Trip Log vs Daily Logs: What's the Difference?
faq_articles.content:
Trip Logs are multi-day narratives that bundle several Daily Logs into one story (for example, a week-long road trip through the Alps). Daily Logs are the building blocks, with detailed technical information for each day.

If you're planning an itinerary, start from the Trip Log. If you're benchmarking consumption or testing charger costs, work from the Daily Logs.

faq_articles.title: Using Collections and Itineraries
faq_articles.content:
Collections let you group related daily logs or trip posts (for example, "DE <25€ Days"). Itineraries are ordered collections, usually representing a future plan like a weekend route.

You can add items to a collection or itinerary directly from the Trip Log, Daily Logs, or Search pages using the "Save to Collection" or "Add to Itinerary" buttons.

faq_articles.title: Saving Posts to Reading Lists
faq_articles.content:
Reading lists are lightweight collections used purely for reading later, such as "Norway Slow & Scenic". Unlike itineraries, they don't affect route planning.

Click the bookmark icon or "Save for Later" on any trip post, then either create a new reading list or add the post to an existing one.

faq_articles.title: Using the Charging Map Route Planner
faq_articles.content:
The Charging Map lets you plan routes between cities (for example, Munich to Vienna) and filter chargers by power, connector type, and network.

You can:
- Set a minimum kW to find only high-power DC fast chargers
- Add chargers to your Trip Notes with custom labels
- Export notes to use in your navigation app.

This is especially useful when scouting two or three specific fast-charging options along a corridor.

faq_articles.title: Commenting and Rating Helpfulness on Trip Posts
faq_articles.content:
Each Trip Log has a comments section where you can leave feedback or questions. Some posts also offer a helpfulness rating (1–5). Use this to indicate how useful you found the information for planning your own trip.

Constructive, specific comments—especially about charging behavior on long days (400 km+), winter conditions, or unexpected detours—are the most valuable.

trips.title: Chamonix Passes Efficiency Day in the Summer Alps
trips.summary: A single-day loop from Chamonix over two mid-altitude passes, focusing on gentle driving and regeneration to see how low consumption can go in the summer Alps.

trips.title: Brenner Pass Economy Run from Innsbruck
trips.summary: A measured autumn climb from Innsbruck to the Brenner Pass and back, testing how careful pacing and preconditioning influence consumption.

trips.title: Snowy Innsbruck Alpine Loop with Chains On Board
trips.summary: A mid-winter day trip from Innsbruck into nearby valleys with fresh snow, documenting how heater use and slush affect consumption.

trips.title: Chur Alpine Pass Loop with Regeneration Focus
trips.summary: A sunny early-summer loop from Chur over two classic Swiss passes, optimizing descents for maximum regeneration and low Wh/km.

trips.title: Salzburg Lake District Economy Day
trips.summary: A gentle loop from Salzburg through the Salzkammergut lakes, keeping speeds moderate to see how low we can push consumption in rolling Alpine foothills.

trips.title: Bavarian Alps & Lakes Relaxed Weekend
trips.summary: A two-day weekend from Munich into the Bavarian Alps, mixing lakeside strolls with easy mountain roads and frequent photo stops.

trips.title: Tyrolean Pass Hopping Weekend
trips.summary: A compact two-day loop from Innsbruck across several Tyrolean passes, balancing scenic views with realistic charging stops.

trips.title: Black Forest Curves & Cafés Weekend
trips.summary: A relaxed two-day weekend through the Black Forest with plenty of twisty roads and coffee stops, ideal for moderate-speed efficiency testing.

trips.title: Oslo Fjord Mini-Break Weekend
trips.summary: A two-day Oslo fjord escape with slow coastal roads, small towns, and just enough driving to test weekend-range planning.

trips.title: Oslo to Geilo Scenic Weekend Road Trip
trips.summary: A weekend from Oslo up to Geilo and back, with mountain viewpoints and slow, winding roads that keep average speed low but enjoyment high.

trips.title: Norway Fjords Grand Loop
trips.summary: A multi-day fjord odyssey with ferries, narrow cliffside roads, and multiple DC fast-charging days, designed as a reference Norway road trip.

trips.title: Geiranger Autumn Colors Slow Day Trip
trips.summary: A single autumn day around Geiranger Fjord, driving slowly between viewpoints and relying on a cabin destination charger overnight.

trips.title: Berlin to Innsbruck Long-Distance Sprint
trips.summary: A demanding long-distance push from Berlin to Innsbruck in one day, leaning heavily on German and Austrian high-power chargers.

trips.title: Arctic Circle Challenge from Trondheim
trips.summary: A late-summer push from Trondheim to the Arctic Circle and back, testing long-distance planning, charger reliability, and fatigue management.

trips.title: Salzburg to Vienna Winter Efficiency Run
trips.summary: A cold December drive from Salzburg to Vienna, focusing on seat heaters, moderate speeds, and charger reliability in snow.

trips.title: Oslo Ski Weekend Winter Efficiency Special
trips.summary: A two-day ski weekend from Oslo, with preconditioning at home and careful heater use to keep winter consumption reasonable.

trips.title: Munich to Salzburg First Snow Shakedown
trips.summary: A pre-holiday test run from Munich to Salzburg in the first proper snow of the season, capturing baseline winter efficiency and charger availability.
